• catering_inquiries (customer-submitted, no location assignment at submission)
Two-admin setup: the schema is prepared. Each admin gets a row in admin_location_assignments scoping them to their location. No schema changes needed when location 2 opens.
The previous approach of using employee_profiles.location_id for admin location scoping was a contradiction:
ADMIN was removed from employee_profiles.role, so admins may not have an employee_profiles row at all.
Admin location scoping must NOT depend on employee_profiles.
user_id FK users(id) -- must have users.role = ADMIN
• Each ADMIN user gets one row per location they manage (usually just one)
• Admin panel endpoint reads admin_location_assignments to scope queries by location_id
• Admin sees only data for their assigned location(s)
• Two admins for two locations = two users with one assignment row each
This is the source of truth for which admin can see which location data.
It is an explicit FK relationship, not a service-layer convention.

Store hours and delivery hours are referenced throughout the PRD for scheduling validation (Sections 2.1, 2.2, 8). Without an explicit schema they cannot be enforced — "schedule within store hours" checks against nothing.
day_of_week ENUM: MON | TUE | WED | THU | FRI | SAT | SUN
hours_type ENUM: STORE | DELIVERY -- STORE = pickup/walk-in; DELIVERY = delivery orders
is_closed boolean default false -- true = closed all day; open/close times ignored
-- Delivery hours may differ from store hours (e.g. delivery stops 21:00, store closes 22:00)
-- Section 26.6 Store Settings Panel maps to this table for admin editing
-- All scheduling validation in Sections 2.1, 2.2, and 8 reads from here
Enables the payout management UI described in Section 17.5. Pay periods are created by admin and linked to all deliveries within that period per driver.
total_deliveries int -- count of DELIVERED orders in period
total_tips_cents int -- sum of driver_tip_cents for period
base_pay_cents int -- configured per-delivery rate x deliveries
driver_payout_order_links (which orders are in a payout)
driver_user_id FK users(id) -- explicit for query clarity; must match driver_payouts.driver_user_id
tip_cents int -- snapshot of driver_tip_cents at time of payout creation
base_pay_cents int -- per-delivery base rate at time of payout
-- UNIQUE (order_id, driver_user_id): one order can appear only once for a given driver across all payout links, regardless of batch
-- Enforces the business rule at DB level, not just service layer
BXGY (Buy X Get Y) is a distinct promo type that requires its own rule payload. The generic promo_code_product_targets tables are not sufficient to define a BXGY rule cleanly.
qualifying_category_id FK menu_categories(id) nullable -- X must be in this category
qualifying_product_id FK menu_items(id) nullable -- or this specific product
required_qty int -- how many of X must be in cart
reward_category_id FK menu_categories(id) nullable -- Y is from this category
reward_product_id FK menu_items(id) nullable -- or this specific product
reward_qty int -- how many of Y are rewarded free
reward_rule ENUM: CHEAPEST | SPECIFIC -- CHEAPEST = cheapest eligible item free
max_uses_per_order int default 1 -- e.g. buy 6 get 2 applies twice to buy 12 get 4
Order attribution (which driver gets paid for which delivery):
• Tip and base pay attributed to the driver who COMPLETED the delivery (final assigned_driver_user_id)
• If a delivery was reassigned, only the final driver earns tip and base pay for that order
• Original driver who was unassigned earns nothing for that order
Order eligibility for a payout batch:
• Only orders with final_status = DELIVERED are included in payout batches — full stop.
• NO_SHOW_DELIVERY orders are NOT included in payout batches. Driver did not complete the delivery.
• If the owner wants to compensate drivers for no-show deliveries, that is a manual adjustment outside this system.
• Tips = $0 for NO_SHOW_DELIVERY regardless (no delivery, no tip)
• Refunded or disputed orders: tip_cents on order is not modified; payout reflects order data at batch creation time
• If an order is refunded AFTER a payout is marked PAID: the payout is NOT retroactively adjusted
Admin must handle any resulting dispute manually outside the system
• One order can only belong to one payout batch per driver — enforced by UNIQUE (order_id, driver_user_id) on driver_payout_order_links
• A payout batch can be regenerated (CANCELLED and recreated) before it is marked PAID
• Once marked PAID: batch is immutable; corrections require a manual adjustment note outside system
• Payout UI tip summary (no batch) can be shown immediately from existing order data
• Payout management (batch create, mark paid) requires driver_payouts table rows to exist
Added on top of defaults when busy mode ON
Grace period for kitchen to adjust ETA on arrival
Changes take effect immediately for new orders. Existing in-progress orders are not retroactively updated.
11.1B New Order Arrival — Configurable Auto-Accept Window
🔔 New Order Arrival — Requirements (not just behaviour)
✅ Required: audible alert when a new order arrives on KDS (must be unmissable in a noisy kitchen)
✅ Required: auto-accept timeout must be configurable per location in location_settings (kds_auto_accept_seconds)

✅ Required: if KDS device is offline or heartbeat has lapsed, do NOT auto-accept — order remains in PLACED status with requires_manual_review = true on the orders row. Admin panel and any online KDS device must show a “Pending Review” alert banner for these orders. No new status is needed — the flag is an operational marker within the existing PLACED state. Flag clearing: requires_manual_review is set to false automatically when a human manually accepts or rejects the order. Alert dismissal in the UI is not sufficient — the flag is cleared by the state transition, not by clicking away a banner.
During the countdown window, kitchen staff can:
— Adjust ETA up or down before accepting
• After timeout with no action: order is auto-accepted at the default ETA
• Auto-acceptance creates an order_status_events entry with source = SYSTEM, action_source = SYSTEM_AUTO_ACCEPT
Owner must configure kds_auto_accept_seconds to their comfort level before launch.
• When ON: all new orders get +X minutes added to ETA
• Customer ordering UI shows updated ETA immediately
• When OFF: new orders revert to standard default ETA
Adjustment buttons on each live order: +5 / +10 / +15 / −5 min
Customer order detail page updates within 3 seconds
Every adjustment logged in order_eta_events (who, when, old + new value)
ETA changes do not change order status
Checkout page shows current default ETA slot (busy-mode-adjusted if active)
Order detail page shows live ETA that updates when KDS staff adjust it
Format: "Ready in approx. 30–40 minutes" or "Ready by 6:45 PM"
• Admin can update pickup and delivery default ETA windows at any time
• Changes take effect immediately on customer ordering UI for new orders
• Existing in-progress orders not affected by global changes
• Busy mode toggle available from KDS header and admin panel
• When busy mode ON: new orders +X min; customer ordering UI reflects update immediately
• KDS +5/+10/+15/-5 buttons update customer order detail page within 3 seconds
• Every ETA adjustment logged in order_eta_events
12. Cancel / Help / Contact Flow & Refund Requests
• "Cancel Order" button visible for exactly 2 minutes after placing
• After 2 minutes: Cancel replaced by Help button
→ Cancellation record written with: cancelled_by_user_id, cancellation_reason = "Customer cancelled within window", cancelled_at, cancellation_source = CUSTOMER_SELF
→ Order stays in history — never deleted
→ Admin can see in Cancelled Orders section with full detail:
Order placed at | Cancelled at | Requested by: Customer | Order type (Pickup/Delivery) | Items | Total
Help button replaces Cancel after the 2-minute window.
Clicking Help shows: "Cancellation might not be possible — your order may already be in preparation. To request a cancellation, please contact us."
"Contact us" button: mobile → phone call | desktop → click-to-call + phone number displayed

If the customer contacts the restaurant via live order chat (Section 15) requesting cancellation, any KDS employee can initiate a cancellation request from the KDS live order settings. The request is sent to Admin for approval. This flow ensures full traceability.
1️⃣ Customer sends cancellation request via live order chat
2️⃣ KDS employee sees the chat message on the KDS order card and can initiate a cancellation request
3️⃣ KDS employee clicks "⋯ Live Order Settings" on the KDS card → "Request Cancel" option
4️⃣ Confirmation modal: "Are you sure you want to cancel this order?" [ Yes ] [ No, go back ]
Shows: fulfillment type (PICKUP / DELIVERY), items, total, customer name
6️⃣ On confirm → cancellation_request record created (status = PENDING) with:
• requested_by_user_id = KDS employee who initiated the cancellation request
• cancellation_source = KDS_CHAT_REQUEST (set when Admin approves the cancellation request)
Order placed at | Requested at | Approved at | Requested by: [KDS employee name] | Approved by: [Admin name] | Reason | Order type | Chat record link | Items | Total
Admin can cancel any order not in a final status from the Admin panel
Admin can cancel directly from the Admin panel (no request needed — Admin is the approving authority). Manager panel cancel also available as an admin-level override.

Any KDS employee (KITCHEN or MANAGER role) can request cancellation from KDS with mandatory reason and full audit trail. The request is pending until Admin approves. Only Admin approval transitions the order to CANCELLED.
cancellation_source = ADMIN for admin panel direct cancel; KDS_CANCEL_REQUEST for any KDS-employee-initiated cancellation request (approved by Admin)
Full detail stored in cancelled order record — stays in history forever
Admin panel has a dedicated "Cancelled Orders" section under Order Management. Every cancellation is visible here regardless of who initiated it.
Two timestamps: when the cancellation was requested (requested_at) and when it was finalized (cancelled_at). For customer self-cancel both are the same.
Who initiated: Customer self / KDS employee name + role / Admin name / System. Shows requested_by_user_id. For customer self-cancel, this is the only actor.
Admin who approved the cancellation (approved_by_user_id). Null for customer self-cancel and system auto-cancel. Shows approval timestamp separately from request timestamp.
None / Pending approval / Issued (if paid order)
Link to live order chat thread if applicable

If a cancelled order has a remaining refundable balance (net_paid_amount_cents > 0), the system must handle the refund. Refunds are admin-approved. Any KDS employee (KITCHEN or MANAGER) can initiate a refund request from the KDS order detail page. KDS initiation does not issue money — it only opens the refund workflow. Only Admin can approve and issue the refund. No role can issue original-payment refunds directly from KDS.

Trigger: Any cancelled order (by any party) with net_paid_amount_cents > 0 must create or update a refund request workflow for the remaining refundable amount. This covers orders with payment_status_summary = PAID as well as PARTIALLY_REFUNDED orders that still have an outstanding balance.

net_paid_amount_cents is a derived value, not a stored column. Compute as: SUM(signed_amount_cents) from order_payments WHERE transaction_type = CAPTURE AND transaction_status = SUCCESS for the order, PLUS SUM(signed_amount_cents) from order_payments WHERE transaction_type = REFUND AND transaction_status = SUCCESS for the same order (refund rows are negative). Refund automation must be based on this derived balance, not on payment_status_summary alone. payment_status_summary is a summary label that can lag behind the real money state; the order_payments table is the source of truth. The refund request amount_cents for a cancellation-triggered refund must equal this derived remaining balance. See also: financial invariant in Section 10.5E (over-refund guard).
1️⃣ System automatically creates a refund_request record (or updates an existing open request) when a cancelled order has net_paid_amount_cents > 0 (covers PAID and PARTIALLY_REFUNDED orders)
2️⃣ Any KDS employee can also manually initiate a refund request from the KDS order detail page (initiation only — KDS cannot approve or issue refunds)
3️⃣ Admin sees a "Refund Requests" section in the admin panel with:
• Order number + customer name + order total + cancellation reason
• Who initiated the refund request + when
4️⃣ Admin selects refund method and approves:
• Refund to original payment method (SHOWN but DISABLED for now — will activate with Stripe/payment gateway)
• Store credit: CREDIT_GRANTED ledger entry → customer_wallets.credit_balance_cents updated
• Original method: order_payments table REFUND entry created → triggers payment gateway call (future)
⚠️ UI Rule: Show Both Options, Disable One
In the admin refund panel, always show both refund method buttons:
[ ✅ Issue as Store Credit ] [ 🔒 Refund to Original Payment — Coming Soon ]
The "Refund to Original Payment" button is visible but disabled with a tooltip:
"This option will be available once online payment processing is configured."
This gives admin a clear picture of what is coming without creating confusion.
• Cancel button visible for exactly 2 minutes after placing, then replaced by Help
• Self-cancel: CANCELLED + cancellation_source = CUSTOMER_SELF + stored in history
• Admin Cancelled Orders section shows all cancellations with full detail
• Any KDS employee can request cancellation via "Live Order Settings" with mandatory reason (Admin approval required before order becomes CANCELLED)
• cancellation_source = KDS_CHAT_REQUEST when initiated via chat (set on Admin approval, not on KDS request submission)
• Chat thread linked to cancellation record for admin visibility
• Cancelled order with net_paid_amount_cents > 0 automatically creates or updates refund_request record for remaining refundable amount
• Admin sees all pending refund requests with full details
• Admin approves refund as store credit (active) or original method (disabled/visible only)
• Store credit approval: CREDIT_GRANTED ledger entry + balance updated
• All refund records store: initiated_by, approved_by, method, amount, timestamps, notes
• Window is 3 minutes after placing (reduced from 5 for operational clarity).
• System does NOT silently edit the order. All additions go through a tracked request.
• A change request is created with status PENDING.
• Admin/Manager can approve or reject (not KDS kitchen staff — see design note below).
• If order has not yet been ACCEPTED, request may auto-approve if add_items_auto_approve_enabled = true in location_settings (default: false).
📌 Design Note: Approval is admin/manager only.
KDS is an operational surface for order fulfilment.
Change request approval is a managerial decision that affects order totals and payment.
Giving kitchen staff approval power creates billing errors and accountability gaps.

Online card orders: payment is authorized at checkout and captured on ACCEPTED. KDS auto-accept can move an order to ACCEPTED within seconds. After capture, there is no simple way to charge the customer more without a separate payment flow. MVP rule: add-item requests for online card orders are only allowed while the order is still PLACED (before capture). If the order is already ACCEPTED or later, reject the add-item request and show the customer: “Your order is already being prepared. Please place a separate order for additional items.” Cash/POS orders: add-item requests are allowed only within the 3-minute window and only while the order is in a non-final, pre-handoff state (PLACED, ACCEPTED, or PREPARING). Orders in READY, OUT_FOR_DELIVERY, PICKED_UP, DELIVERED, NO_SHOW_CUSTOMER, NO_SHOW_DRIVER, or CANCELLED must reject add-item requests even if the 3-minute window has not elapsed — the updated total is collected at pickup. Store credit orders: add-item delta is deducted from wallet on approval; if wallet balance is insufficient, reject the request. Future: a real delta-charge flow (incremental auth or second charge) can replace this rule once the payment gateway supports it.
1️⃣ Within 3 minutes: "Add Items" button visible on active order detail page
2️⃣ Tapping opens a mini cart → customer selects items to add → confirms
3️⃣ System creates order_change_request with status PENDING
4️⃣ Customer sees: "Your request to add items is pending approval."
5️⃣ Approved: items appended, totals updated, customer UI updates
6️⃣ Rejected: customer sees rejection reason (see 13.3) + "Please place a new order."
1️⃣ Admin sees "Pending Add-Item Requests" badge in admin panel notification area
2️⃣ Admin opens request: sees original order + requested items + customer info
3️⃣ Approve: items added with full snapshots, totals recalculated, customer notified
4️⃣ Reject: admin must enter a mandatory rejection reason (free text, min 5 chars)
• Displayed to the customer: "Your request was not approved: [reason]"
• Stored in admin panel under "Cancelled Add-On Requests" with:
— Customer name | Original order | Requested items | Rejection reason | Rejected by | Time
5️⃣ Both outcomes logged: resolved_by_user_id + resolved_at + reason
• "Add Items" button visible for 3 minutes after placing, then disappears
• order_change_request always created — never a silent mutation
• Approval is admin/manager only — KDS kitchen staff cannot approve
• Rejection reason shown to customer and stored in admin "Cancelled Add-On Requests"
• Approval: items appended with snapshots, totals updated, customer notified
• Both outcomes logged with resolver id, timestamp, and reason

Support chat tied to a specific live order. Not a general inbox — scoped to active orders only.
✅ OPEN: PLACED | ACCEPTED | PREPARING | READY | OUT_FOR_DELIVERY
🔒 READ-ONLY: DELIVERED | PICKED_UP | NO_SHOW_PICKUP | NO_SHOW_DELIVERY | CANCELLED
⚠️ No new messages after finalization. Existing messages remain visible.
Each message records: who sent it (user + surface), the message body, when it was sent, and whether it is a system-generated message. Staff-only visibility is supported for internal notes.
System messages supported (auto-sent on status changes)
Rate limiting: max 5 messages per minute per user
Staff/admin can flag messages as STAFF_ONLY
Chat button on active order detail page with unread badge
Read-only mode labelled: "This order is complete. Chat is now closed."
Chat panel on KDS order card for live orders
Staff can reply directly from KDS
• Chat open for PLACED through OUT_FOR_DELIVERY
• Read-only on finalization; no new messages accepted
• Every message stored with sender identity, surface, timestamp, and linkage to the parent order
• Rate limiting applied (5 msg/min per user)
• Unread badge on customer page; unread highlight on KDS

Unread state is tracked per side (customer vs staff), not per individual staff member. When a customer sends a message, all staff surfaces (KDS, Manager, Admin) show unread for that order. When any staff member opens the conversation, unread clears for all staff on that thread. When staff sends a message, the customer sees unread on their order detail page. When the customer opens the conversation, customer unread clears. There is no per-staff-member read tracking in MVP, and no user-facing “seen by X” read receipts. Unread state drives the customer-page badge and KDS highlight only. The canonical storage for unread behavior is chat_side_read_states (PK: order_id + reader_side). reader_side is CUSTOMER or STAFF. When any staff member opens the conversation, the service must advance the STAFF-side cursor and clear staff unread for all staff views of that order. chat_read_states (PK: order_id + user_id) is retained as an optional per-user audit/helper layer for analytics and future per-user tracking. See the Schema Spec for column details.

A flexible promo engine supporting multiple discount types with clear restriction rules. Stacking is disabled by default to prevent abuse.
For launch: promos are global. A promo created at location 1 is also valid at location 2.
• % discount and fixed-amount promos apply at any location the customer orders from.
• A BXGY promo referencing a category_id only at location 1 silently does not apply at location 2
if that category does not exist there. No error — just no discount. This is correct behavior.
• Admin must manually deactivate a promo if it should not apply at a second location.

promo_code_location_targets (schema defined — build before location 2 opens) id UUID PK promo_code_id FK promo_codes(id) location_id FK locations(id) -- UNIQUE: (promo_code_id, location_id) -- When this table has rows for a promo: promo applies ONLY at listed locations. -- When this table has NO rows for a promo: promo applies globally (backward-compatible default). -- This is an additive change. No existing promo_codes columns are modified. -- Checkout promo validation must JOIN this table when rows exist for the code.
This will be an additive schema change — no changes to existing promo_codes table needed.

🚨 LOCATION-2 PREREQUISITE — Promo Location Scoping. The promo_code_location_targets schema is now defined above. Until it is implemented and deployed, all promos apply at every location. This is an operational risk, not just inconvenience: discounts can apply at the wrong store, BXGY promos can silently no-op where target categories do not exist, and analytics are cross-location by default. This limitation is acceptable for a single-location launch only. If location 2 is planned within the near term, promo_code_location_targets MUST be built before location 2 accepts orders — treat it as a location-2 launch prerequisite, not a backlog item. Without it: staff must manually audit every active promo before location 2 opens, promo analytics will mix locations, and wrong-store discounts are a certainty. Do not open location 2 with global promos and hope humans will catch the edge cases — they will not.
Buy 3 lb wings, get 1 lb free
Every promo code can be configured with the following restrictions. Without these, promos will be abused in the first week.
Valid for specific days of the week (e.g. Monday only)
Valid for specific time window (e.g. 11 AM – 3 PM)
Valid for specific menu categories or product IDs
First-order only (validated against first paid completed order)
Cannot combine with other offers (stackable = false, the default)
Total usage limit across all customers
• Applies only to first eligible paid order (not first order placed — to prevent abandoned order abuse)
• Can be restricted to delivery-only, pickup-only, or both
• Optional max discount cap and minimum subtotal
• Controlled as a promo code (is_first_order_only = true) — not a hardcoded rule
• Admin can disable or change the percentage at any time without code changes
📌 Canonical First-Order Query Rule — must be used everywhere:
A qualifying prior order = same user_id, AND final_status IN (PICKED_UP, DELIVERED),
net_paid_amount_cents is a DERIVED value — not a stored column.
Compute it as: SUM of signed_amount_cents from order_payments table where order_id matches AND transaction_type = CAPTURE AND transaction_status = SUCCESS
MINUS SUM of amount_cents from payments where type = REFUND for same order
PARTIALLY_REFUNDED = query payments to compute exact net
Disqualifying cases (order does NOT count as the first eligible paid order):
• Cancelled order (including cancelled after capture — payment was reversed)
• Fully refunded order (payment_status_summary = REFUNDED — net paid = 0)
• POS order with payment_method = STORE_CREDIT and credit was fully reversed
Qualifying cases (order counts as prior eligible order):
• Completed, paid, non-refunded order (PICKED_UP or DELIVERED, payment_status_summary = PAID)
• POS cash order, POS card order, online card order — all count if net paid > 0
When multiple discount types apply, apply in this exact order:
2️⃣ Subtotal promotions (% off, fixed amount off)
This order prevents compounding abuse and produces predictable results.

All monetary values are stored as integer cents. No floats anywhere in the money path. Percentage discounts: calculated in decimal, then rounded half-up to the nearest cent (e.g. 10% off $13.57 = $1.357 → $1.36 discount). Tax: calculated on the discounted subtotal after all promos, then rounded half-up to the nearest cent. Rounding level: rounding happens at the order level, not per line item — sum all line amounts in exact cents first, apply percentage to the sum, then round once. This prevents cumulative per-line rounding drift. The backend is the source of truth. If the frontend total differs by even 1 cent, the backend total wins and the frontend must re-sync before confirming payment.
Worked Example 1 — Percentage discount + tax (pickup order)
Worked Example 2 — Delivery order with $5 fixed discount + tip + delivery fee
HST 13% on discounted subtotal: 2298 × 0.13 = 298.74 → round half-up → 299

Note: tax is calculated on discounted subtotal + delivery fee (delivery fees are taxable for prepared food in Ontario; tax_delivery_fee = true). Tip is NOT taxable (tax_tip = false). These examples are the canonical test cases — frontend, backend, and QA must all produce the same cent values.

✅ TAX CONTRACT FROZEN — Launch location: London, Ontario. Tax: HST 13% (tax_rate_bps = 1300, tax_snapshot_label = ONTARIO_HST_13). Currency: CAD. Taxable base: discounted_subtotal_cents + delivery_fee_cents (if tax_delivery_fee = true) + driver_tip_cents (if tax_tip = true). Launch defaults: tax_delivery_fee = true (delivery fees ARE taxable for prepared food in Ontario), tax_tip = false (tips NOT taxable), discounts_reduce_taxable_base = true. Formula: tax_amount_cents = round_half_up(taxable_base × tax_rate_bps / 10000). Rounding: round half-up, once, on the total taxable base. Do NOT round per line item. Per-order snapshot: tax_rate_bps, tax_delivery_fee_applied, tax_tip_applied, and tax_snapshot_label are frozen at checkout on every order. Receipts, refunds, and reports MUST use the order-level snapshot, never current config. pricing_snapshot_json stores the full frozen rule inputs for historical reconstruction. Daily tax reporting: daily_tax_summary table (PK: business_date + location_id) stores per-location aggregates: orders_count, taxable_sales_cents, tax_collected_cents, refund_tax_reversed_cents, net_tax_cents.
Service updates this table when orders complete or refunds issue. Receipts and refunds still use per-order snapshots; this table is for admin dashboard and reporting only.
Total revenue from orders that used the promo
All analytics admin-only, not shown to customers
• Valid promo code applied at checkout: discount shown + order_discounts row created
• Expired, over-limit, or ineligible codes: clear error message shown
• Two codes cannot be stacked if stackable = false on either
• First-order code blocked for returning customers
• BXGY promo auto-detects qualifying cart items and applies without customer input
• Admin can activate, deactivate, or edit promo codes at any time
• Promo analytics visible in admin panel
Admin can issue store credit to customers as a service recovery tool. Credit is tracked in a ledger, not a single mutable number.
Admin or Manager opens a customer profile and clicks "Issue Credit"
Enters amount, selects reason (wrong order / missing item / service recovery / promo goodwill / other)
Creates CREDIT_GRANTED ledger entry + updates cached credit_balance_cents
Customer sees available credit balance on checkout
Credit applied after all promos and before final total
Credit cannot reduce payable amount below $0 (excess credit stays on balance)
CREDIT_USED ledger entry created on successful checkout
Credit history visible under account settings
Credits with an expiry date automatically deactivate at expiry (CREDIT_EXPIRED ledger entry)
Admin can reverse a mistaken credit grant by issuing a CREDIT_REVERSED entry
Ledger is immutable: no editing rows, only adding entries
Full audit trail: every action records who, when, and why

When admin resolves a support ticket (Section 25) with "Store Credit" as the resolution type, the credit issuance flow from 19.1 is triggered directly from the ticket resolution screen. No duplicate data entry.
19.5 Store Credit & Split Payment Accounting Policy
💳 Accounting Rules — Credit Application, Refund Reversal & Expiry
2️⃣ Subtotal promos (% off, fixed amount off)
5️⃣ Store credit applied (up to remaining balance, cannot go below $0)
6️⃣ Remaining amount charged to card or cash
Refund reversal order (when refunding a split credit + card payment):
• Store credit portion → re-credited to customer wallet first (CREDIT_GRANTED ledger entry)
• Card portion → refunded to original card second (via payment gateway, future)
• Rationale: credit was applied last at purchase; reversed first on refund (LIFO)
• Credits with expiry dates are evaluated at checkout: expired credits cannot be applied
• Multiple credits: oldest expiry first (FIFO expiry consumption)
• Non-expiring credits consumed after all expiring credits
• Credit expiry is checked at checkout server-side, not just at display time
• If credit expires between cart build and checkout submission: remove from total, show banner
• Never allow expired credit to be applied even if UI displayed it as valid
• Admin can issue any amount; Manager can issue up to manager_credit_limit_cents (location_settings)
• Credit creates CREDIT_GRANTED ledger entry + updates cached balance
• Customer sees credit balance at checkout and can toggle to apply
• Credit cannot make payable amount negative
• Admin can reverse credit (CREDIT_REVERSED); ledger never edited in place
• Refund reversal: credit portion re-credited first, card portion second
• Expiry checked server-side at checkout; expired credit never applied

Employees can create orders from an internal POS-style screen. Walk-in and phone orders go through the same order lifecycle as online orders — no separate kitchen process.
order_source canonical values (standardised — use these everywhere):
• ONLINE — placed by customer on website
• POS — created by employee at in-store register
• PHONE — called in, entered by employee
⚠️ Note: IN_STORE and ADMIN_CREATED are deprecated references. Use POS and ADMIN respectively everywhere in code.
The in-store POS screen must ONLY be accessible from the restaurant's in-store WiFi network.
This prevents employees or drivers from accessing the POS remotely or from personal networks.
• Backend: POS-specific endpoints are IP-allowlisted to the store's static IP address
• If request comes from outside the store IP range: 403 Forbidden with message "POS access is restricted to in-store network only"
• Each location has its own registered IP range in location_settings
• This is configured once per location at setup — no per-employee configuration needed
• Driver availability state (set to AVAILABLE on clock-in via timeclock, NOT a manual toggle — see Section 23.6)
• Admin panel (full auth via credentials, accessible from anywhere)
2️⃣ Navigates to POS screen (only reachable on store network)
4️⃣ System identifies employee, validates role, opens POS screen

5️⃣ POS login opens the order screen only. It does NOT clock the employee in or change driver availability. Driver availability is set exclusively by the timeclock (Section 23.6): clock-in → AVAILABLE, clock-out → OFF_SHIFT. Employee must clock in separately after POS login to appear in the KDS driver picker.
• Codes are stored hashed (bcrypt) — never plain text. Verification uses hash comparison only.
• Rate limiting: max 5 failed attempts per device per 10 minutes before lockout
• On lockout: 10-minute cooldown enforced server-side; message: "Too many attempts. Please wait."
• Every failed attempt is logged in admin_audit_logs (action = POS_LOGIN_FAIL, device_id, timestamp)
• Deactivated employee codes do not unlock POS even if code is correct (is_active check first)
• Code reuse policy: when an employee is deactivated, their code cannot be reassigned for 30 days
• Opening the employee POS order screen
• Identifying who created a walk-in order
• Editing financial settings, issuing credits, managing promos
• Any action requiring manager or admin permission (these require full authentication)
1️⃣ Employee connects to store WiFi + enters 5-digit code → POS screen opens
2️⃣ Employee selects items from menu (same menu as online)
3️⃣ Customizations applied (wings builder, modifiers, ingredient removal, add-ons)

4️⃣ Two separate inputs (do not combine into one “order type” field): Fulfillment type: Pickup | Delivery Order source: POS (walk-in) | PHONE (called in) These map directly to orders.fulfillment_type and orders.order_source as defined in Section 10.5.
5️⃣ Payment method selected: Cash | Card | Store Credit
6️⃣ Order submitted → enters KDS lifecycle (PLACED → ACCEPTED → PREPARING → READY)
8️⃣ Receipt printed automatically for cash payments
Card / Debit: receipt prints, drawer stays closed
Store Credit: credit deducted from customer wallet

Split payment: partial cash + partial card. Data model supports this now via multiple order_payments rows (see §10.5E). Split payment is not exposed in MVP POS UX, but the backend transaction model supports it for forward compatibility.
orders.payment_method is a summary field for reporting and UI. Source of truth for money movement = order_payments rows (see §10.5E canonical rule)
Manager and Admin can apply manual discounts on walk-in orders
Cashier can only apply approved promo codes
Every manual discount logs: applied_by_user_id, reason, amount
• POS screen only accessible from store WiFi (IP allowlist enforced server-side)
• Outside store network: 403 Forbidden with clear message
• Employee enters 5-digit code after connecting to store WiFi
• Driver employees set to AVAILABLE on clock-in (timeclock), not on POS login; OFF_SHIFT on clock-out (Section 23.6)

Replaces Clover's TimeClock module. Covers employee profiles, roles, clock in/out, breaks, and timesheet administration.
MANAGER | CASHIER | KITCHEN | DRIVER (ADMIN is NOT in this list — see Section 10.0 canonical auth rule)
Inactive employees cannot use POS or timeclock
• Cannot clock in twice without clocking out first
• Cannot start break while already on break
• Cannot end break if no break is active
• Cannot clock out while on break (system auto-ends break with confirmation prompt)
• All actions timestamped and stored in employee_shifts + employee_breaks
Admin can view timesheet data per employee with the following ranges:
Per employee: clock-in time, clock-out time, break records, total worked, total break time, net worked time
Summary view: currently clocked in / out / on break
Since someone will always forget to clock out:
• Admin / Manager can edit a punch record
• Can add missing clock-out, correct break times, adjust timestamps
• Every correction must log: old value | new value | edited_by | reason | edited_at
• Corrections visible in employee timesheet history
Cashier attribution: which orders were created by which internal user (via created_by_user_id)
Sales handled by employee (walk-in orders count + value)
Shift notes: employee or manager can add a note per shift
Attendance history with filters: date range, role, missed punches, overtime candidates
Overtime flag when weekly hours exceed a configured threshold (flag only, no payroll calculation)
23.6 Driver Availability — Controlled by Clock-In / Clock-Out

Employees who are also drivers have their driver availability_status automatically managed by timeclock actions. This ensures the KDS driver picker and future driver app always reflect who is actually on shift.
On Clock-In (via POS on store WiFi):
→ Driver now appears in KDS assignment picker
→ Future driver app endpoint returns: "You are now on shift and available"
→ If driver is currently on a delivery: clock-out blocked until delivery is completed or reassigned
→ Driver disappears from KDS assignment picker immediately
→ Future driver app endpoint returns: "You are off shift. No new deliveries will be assigned."
• All drivers created with is_active = true and availability_status = OFF_SHIFT
• Drivers do not manually toggle their own availability in MVP
• The timeclock is the single source of truth for availability
• Admin can manually override availability_status to AVAILABLE, UNAVAILABLE, or OFF_SHIFT from the driver profile page if needed (never ON_DELIVERY — that is system-derived only)
Important: This only applies to employees with role = DRIVER.
Regular KITCHEN / CASHIER employees are not affected by driver state logic.
• Employee profile stores all required fields including 5-digit code (unique)
• Roles enforced: different permission levels per role
• Clock in/out, start/end break all timestamped and stored
• Clock rules enforced: cannot double clock-in, cannot double start break, etc.
• Admin can view daily/weekly/monthly hours per employee
• Admin/Manager can edit punches with reason; every correction logged
• Cashier attribution: walk-in orders show which employee created them
• DRIVER role: clock-in sets availability_status = AVAILABLE; clock-out sets OFF_SHIFT
• DRIVER clock-out blocked if delivery in progress until resolved
• Driver only visible in KDS picker when availability_status = AVAILABLE

Physical hardware integration for in-store POS. The recommended architecture is a browser-based POS + local print agent running on the in-store machine. Receipt printer is the bridge between software and cash drawer.
• Cash drawer plugged into thermal receipt printer
• Receipt printer plugged into the in-store computer
• POS screen runs in browser on that computer
• A small local print agent (Node service) runs on the same machine
Order paid → backend notifies frontend → frontend sends to local print agent
Print agent: prints receipt + sends drawer-open command to printer + logs event
Order paid → receipt prints → drawer stays closed
• No-sale open (manager approval required + reason logged)
• Any button press without proper authorization
Every drawer open event is logged in cash_drawer_events.
1️⃣ Start of shift: employee opens register session, enters opening cash amount
2️⃣ During shift: all cash orders, refunds, and drawer events are tracked
3️⃣ End of shift: employee enters actual counted cash
4️⃣ System shows: expected cash | actual cash | over/short difference
5️⃣ Manager approves closure and reconciles (status → RECONCILED)
6️⃣ Notes field for any discrepancy explanation
Order number, fulfillment type (Pickup / Delivery), and order source (Online / POS / Phone / Admin)
Item breakdown with prices, quantities, modifiers
Subtotal, tax, delivery fee (if applicable), tip (if applicable)
List available printers + set default receipt printer
Print + open drawer in one action
Test print button + test drawer open button
Return success/failure status to POS UI
Offline queue for short network interruptions
• Cash payment: receipt prints + drawer opens + cash_drawer_events row created
• Card payment: receipt prints + drawer stays closed
• No-sale drawer open requires manager approval + reason logged
• Register sessions track opening cash, closing cash, expected, actual, difference
• Receipt includes all required fields per 24.4
• All drawer events logged in cash_drawer_events
• Only registered devices with valid API token can receive print jobs
• Device heartbeat required; offline/expired devices cannot receive jobs
Without a device trust model, any machine on the store WiFi could impersonate a printer terminal. This section defines how devices are authenticated and bound to a location.
1️⃣ Admin registers a new device from the admin panel: name, type, station_code, location
2️⃣ System generates a unique API token for that device
3️⃣ Token is shown once in the UI and never again — admin copies it to the local print agent config
4️⃣ api_token_hash stored in devices table (bcrypt hash; raw token never stored)
5️⃣ Device is now registered and active
Device Authentication (every request from print agent):
• Local print agent sends its API token as a Bearer token in every request
• Backend validates token hash against the devices table
• If token does not match a registered active device: 401 Unauthorized
• Device must also be is_active = true and location_id must match the order's location_id
• Local print agent pings a heartbeat endpoint every 60 seconds
• Backend updates devices.last_seen_at on each ping
• If last_seen_at > 5 minutes ago: device is considered offline
• Offline device: print jobs queued but not dispatched until device comes back online
• KDS shows "Printer offline" badge if assigned printer is offline
• Admin can rotate a device token from the devices admin page at any time
• Rotating generates a new token (shown once); immediately invalidates the old hash in DB
• The old print agent receives 401 on its next request and must be reconfigured with the new token
• One device has exactly one active token at a time — no multi-token support
• If token is suspected leaked: rotate immediately; old token is dead the moment new hash is saved
• Deactivating a device (is_active = false) also invalidates its token without rotation
• WiFi restriction (Section 22.2) limits which machines can reach POS endpoints at the IP layer
• Device tokens add a second layer: even on the correct WiFi, unknown machines cannot print
• Together: physical location restriction + cryptographic device identity
Schema update: add api_token_hash and device_registered_at to devices table:
api_token_hash text nullable -- bcrypt hash of the device API token; raw token shown once at registration
device_registered_at timestamptz nullable -- when the token was first issued
token_last_used_at timestamptz nullable -- updated on each authenticated request

Every registered device has exactly one type from this enum. Do not invent synonyms (KITCHEN_TABLET, POS_IPAD, CLOCKIN_TERMINAL, PRINTER_AGENT). If a physical device serves multiple roles, register it once under its primary role and document the secondary role in metadata_json. This enum is frozen and must match the SQL CHECK on devices.device_type.
Canonical enum: KDS_SCREEN | POS_TERMINAL | RECEIPT_PRINTER | CASH_DRAWER | TIMECLOCK_TERMINAL

Operational kitchen/order-management display. Runs the KDS web app. Receives order queue via WebSocket. Used for: accept/reject orders, status transitions, ETA adjustments, driver assignment, delivery controls, chat, and all operational actions defined in §7.5. Auth: employee session (KITCHEN or MANAGER role). Heartbeat: yes (offline = “KDS offline” alert in admin). Token: device API token for heartbeat; employee auth for actions.

Order-entry and payment surface for walk-in and phone orders (§22). Runs the POS web app. Used for: creating orders, applying coupons/discounts, collecting payment, printing receipts. Auth: employee session (CASHIER, MANAGER, or ADMIN role) + WiFi IP allowlist (§22.2). Heartbeat: optional but recommended for device health monitoring. Token: device API token for registration; employee PIN for quick-auth.

Trusted print agent device. Runs the local Node print agent (§24.5). Used for: receipt printing, kitchen ticket printing, drawer-open commands. Auth: device API token (Bearer token on every request). Heartbeat: required (60-second ping; > 5 min stale = offline). Offline behavior: print jobs queued server-side until device reconnects. One printer may serve receipts and kitchen tickets depending on station_key config.

Cash drawer hardware paired to a register or POS station. Controlled via print agent commands (§24.2). Used for: drawer open on cash sale, no-sale open (manager approval required), safe drop recording. Auth: inherits trust from paired POS/print agent. Not independently authenticated — drawer commands flow through the print agent. Paired to register via station_key.

Employee punch-in/punch-out surface (§23). Used for: clock in, clock out, break start/end, driver availability sync (clock in = AVAILABLE, clock out = OFF_SHIFT). Auth: device API token + employee PIN for quick-auth + WiFi IP allowlist. Heartbeat: optional. Driver state linkage: timeclock is the MVP
source of truth for driver availability (§17). A timeclock device may be a dedicated tablet or share hardware with a POS terminal — the device_type determines which route permissions apply.
Why this matters for trust and auth:
• Token issuance: each device gets its own API token at registration. One device = one token = one device_type. No sharing.
• Route permissions: device_type determines which API routes the device can reach. A RECEIPT_PRINTER cannot call KDS status-change endpoints. A TIMECLOCK_TERMINAL cannot create orders.
• Audit trail: admin_audit_logs and device activity logs reference device_type + device_id so incident response can identify exactly which surface/hardware was involved.
• Revocation: deactivating or rotating a device token is scoped to exactly one registered device. No cascading breakage across other surfaces.

• Hardware pairing: station_key links a device to a physical station (e.g. “Front Counter”). CASH_DRAWER and RECEIPT_PRINTER at the same station share a station_key so the system knows which drawer opens when which printer fires.
Structured post-order complaint and issue tracking system. Replaces ad-hoc DM and phone complaint handling with a trackable workflow.
🔒 Complaint Funnel — Two Paths, Both Explicit
Customer issue report → support_ticket → support_ticket_resolutions → (optional) refund_request or credit
Every complaint from a customer goes through a support_ticket. No exceptions.
Paid order cancelled → refund_request auto-created with support_ticket_id = NULL
No support ticket is required for a refund triggered by order cancellation.
This is an operational flow, not a complaint resolution flow.
These two paths are separate and both valid. The schema supports both:
• refund_requests.support_ticket_id is nullable (NULL = cancellation-driven, set = ticket-driven)
• "Single complaint funnel" means all complaints go through tickets, not that all refunds do.
The ticket storage and admin resolution system is already fully defined in this section.
After an order reaches a final status (DELIVERED or PICKED_UP), customer can report an issue from the order detail page.
Issue Types — Customer UI Label → DB Enum
• Delivery issue → DELIVERY_ISSUE (late delivery, wrong address, package damaged)
• Driver issue → DRIVER_ISSUE (rude, unprofessional, safety concern — separate from delivery logistics)
DELIVERY_ISSUE covers logistics: late, wrong address, missing item from driver.
DRIVER_ISSUE covers behaviour: rude, unprofessional, safety concern about the driver specifically.
Admin routes DRIVER_ISSUE tickets to driver management review. DELIVERY_ISSUE goes to ops/kitchen.
Customer selects issue type + optional description
Ticket created with status OPEN + linked to order_id
Customer sees: "Your issue has been reported. We'll respond shortly."
Admin can resolve a ticket with:
• Store credit — triggers Section 19 credit issuance flow
• Partial refund — creates refund_request with support_ticket_id set
• Full refund — creates refund_request with support_ticket_id set
• Follow-up required — ticket stays open for further action
Resolution must include a resolution note. Ticket history is preserved — never deleted.
🔄 Replacement Order — What Happens When Admin Selects "Replacement"
1️⃣ Admin clicks "Create Replacement Order" from the ticket resolution screen

2️⃣ System creates a new order pre-populated with original items, address, and phone. Replacement is a fixed clone of the original order — admin cannot swap items, add extras, or change quantities during creation. If the customer needs different items, admin must issue store credit or a separate comp instead. This prevents value-delta disputes and keeps replacement orders auditable as exact redeliveries.
3️⃣ Order fields: order_source = ADMIN | fulfillment_type = same as original
payment_method = WAIVED (replacement is free by default)
payment_status_summary = PAID (no money collected; $0 order is financially settled)
4️⃣ created_by_user_id = admin who initiated the replacement
5️⃣ support_ticket_resolutions row created with resolution_type = REPLACEMENT and replacement_order_id = new order id
6️⃣ Replacement enters the normal KDS lifecycle (PLACED → ACCEPTED → ...)
• payment_method = WAIVED → counts as $0 revenue in sales reports
• Flagged as comp/service-recovery in reporting by filtering: order_source = ADMIN AND payment_method = WAIVED
• Can be filtered separately in admin reporting (order_source = ADMIN + payment_method = WAIVED)
Can replacement orders be refunded or credited?
• A WAIVED order has no payment to refund.
• If the replacement itself is also wrong, admin must open a new support ticket.
• Store credit can be issued on top of a replacement if warranted (via credit issuance, not a refund).
📌 What resolution combinations are allowed on one ticket:
• Store credit + Replacement (admin discretion; two separate actions on the ticket)
• Full refund + Replacement (would comp both money and food)
• Full refund + Store credit on the same ticket
• Multiple refund_requests for the same full refund amount on one ticket
Closing the financial loop — RESOLVED → CLOSED:
• Ticket can move to RESOLVED only when all financial actions are ISSUED (not just APPROVED)
Path A: Admin explicitly marks CLOSED at any time after RESOLVED (no time minimum)
Path B: Auto-close after support_ticket_autoclose_days if no admin action (see location_settings)
• Set support_ticket_autoclose_days = 0 to disable auto-close (Path B off; Path A only)
• Auto-close writes support_ticket_events: event_type = STATUS_CHANGE, performed_by = NULL, note = “System auto-close after configured grace period”
• Once CLOSED: cannot be reopened. Admin must open a new ticket if the issue recurs.

Support tickets use two separate concepts. Do not invent synonyms, combine them, or create compound states like RESOLVED_REFUND. If this vocabulary drifts across schema, UI, analytics, or filters, reporting will break and nobody will enjoy the resulting archaeology.
Concept A: Ticket Status (lifecycle state — where is this ticket now?)
Canonical enum: OPEN | IN_REVIEW | WAITING_ON_CUSTOMER | RESOLVED | CLOSED
• OPEN: Ticket received, no staff action taken yet
• IN_REVIEW: Admin acknowledged, investigation or action underway
• WAITING_ON_CUSTOMER: Action taken, awaiting customer response or confirmation
• RESOLVED: All financial actions issued, awaiting close confirmation or grace period
• CLOSED: Permanently sealed. Cannot reopen. New ticket required.
Lives on: support_tickets.status. This is the current lifecycle state. Source of truth for filtering, queues, and dashboards.
Concept B: Resolution Type (outcome — what action was taken to resolve this ticket?)
Canonical enum: NO_ACTION | STORE_CREDIT | PARTIAL_REFUND | FULL_REFUND | REPLACEMENT | FOLLOW_UP
• STORE_CREDIT: Credit issued to customer wallet
• FOLLOW_UP: Ticket stays open, further investigation or action required

Lives on: support_ticket_resolutions.resolution_type (one row per resolution action). A ticket can have multiple resolution rows (e.g. STORE_CREDIT + REPLACEMENT). Forbidden combos: FULL_REFUND + REPLACEMENT, FULL_REFUND + STORE_CREDIT on the same ticket.
Summary field on support_tickets (convenience, not source of truth)

support_tickets.resolution_type is a nullable summary convenience field for filtering, badges, dashboards, and quick display (ticket_resolution_type enum — same canonical enum as support_ticket_resolutions). NULL means no resolution has been recorded yet. NO_ACTION is a real business decision, not a placeholder for nothing chosen. It reflects the latest/primary resolution action. Update rule: when a new support_ticket_resolutions row is created, update support_tickets.resolution_type to match. If the summary field and the resolution rows disagree, the rows win. This is the same two-tier pattern used for orders.payment_method vs order_payments (see §10.5E).

Event rows log transitions and notes: STATUS_CHANGE, RESOLUTION_SET, REOPENED, NOTE_ADDED (PRIORITY_CHANGE retained for forward compatibility). These are history. They do not define new state vocabulary. from_value and to_value are plain text describing what changed, not typed enums. Source of truth for current state = support_tickets.status. Source of truth for resolution detail = support_ticket_resolutions rows. support_tickets.ticket_type is the canonical issue classification field. priority is not a launch-scope field unless explicitly added later. Resolution notes belong on support_ticket_resolutions.note or support_ticket_messages, not as a separate column on support_tickets.
OPEN: Ticket received, no action taken yet
IN_REVIEW: Admin acknowledged, investigation or action underway
WAITING_ON_CUSTOMER: Action taken, awaiting customer response or confirmation
RESOLVED: All financial actions ISSUED (refund/credit/replacement created and fulfilled)
Ticket can be reopened from RESOLVED if customer reports the issue persists.
CLOSED: Admin explicitly marks closed after customer acknowledgment or after grace period.
Auto-close after X days is configurable per location in location_settings.
Cannot be reopened once CLOSED — a new ticket must be opened.
Worked Example — Support Ticket Lifecycle (RESOLVED vs CLOSED)
Scenario A — Manual close (admin confirms customer satisfied)
1. Customer reports WRONG_ITEM on Order #1042 → ticket OPEN
(ticket stays RESOLVED — customer can still reply)
4. Admin confirms customer happy, clicks Close → CLOSED (permanent)
Scenario B — Auto-close (no admin action after resolution)
1. Customer reports COLD_FOOD on Order #1098 → ticket OPEN
3. Replacement delivered, all financial actions ISSUED → RESOLVED
4. 14 days pass, no further action → CLOSED (auto)
support_ticket_events row: performed_by = NULL, note = “System auto-close”
Scenario C — Reopen from RESOLVED (issue persists)
1. Ticket reaches RESOLVED after store credit issued
3. Admin reopens ticket → back to IN_REVIEW
(allowed from RESOLVED; NOT allowed from CLOSED)

Key distinction: RESOLVED means “we did our part, waiting to confirm customer is satisfied.” CLOSED means “done, permanently sealed.” QA, backend, and admin UI must all treat this boundary consistently.
Staff can add internal notes (is_internal_note = true, not visible to customer)
Customer-visible replies sent through ticket message thread
• Customer can report issue after DELIVERED or PICKED_UP orders
• Ticket linked to order_id and stored in support_tickets table
• Admin can update status, add notes, and send replies
• Resolution types: no action / credit / partial refund / full refund / replacement / follow-up
• "Store Credit" resolution triggers credit issuance (Section 19) directly
• Replacement order: WAIVED payment, order_source = ADMIN, support_ticket_resolutions.replacement_order_id set
• Full refund + Replacement not allowed on same ticket (double-recovery)
• RESOLVED requires all financial actions ISSUED; CLOSED requires either admin confirmation or configured system auto-close after RESOLVED (support_ticket_autoclose_days)
• Internal notes not visible to customer
• Ticket history preserved; never deleted; every status change writes support_ticket_events
The admin panel is the command centre for the restaurant. It must surface actionable information at a glance and support efficient day-to-day management.
Count of live orders currently in KDS
Gross sales + order count for today
Count of items at or below reorder threshold + link to restock list
Open / Closed status of each active register

All reports and dashboards below must be built from canonical operational tables, immutable event tables, and order-item snapshots — never from widget-specific or report-shaped tables. Do not create denormalized reporting tables that duplicate the truth stored in orders, order_status_events, payments, customer_credit_ledger, or inventory_adjustments. Report-shaped tables age badly, drift from operational data, and make future product changes harder. If a dashboard needs aggregated data, compute it at query time or via materialized views that refresh from the canonical source.
Sales today / this week / this month
Order count and average order value
Revenue split: payment method (cash / card / credit)
Fulfillment type split: Pickup / Delivery. Order source split: Online / POS / Phone / Admin
Best sellers by quantity and revenue
Modifier popularity (which sauces, sides, add-ons most selected)
Every user in the system has a role that determines what they can see and do.
Full access within their assigned location(s) as defined in admin_location_assignments. Multi-location access requires multiple assignment rows.

All KDS actions (accept, request cancellation with mandatory reason, set preparing, set ready, no-show, ETA adjust, initiate refund requests, assign driver, approve busy-driver override, start delivery, mark delivered via PIN flow, mark no-show delivery — these are available to all KDS employees; cancellation and refund requests require Admin approval). Additional manager/admin capabilities: Approve no-sale drawer open • Edit employee punches • Issue store credits up to a configured limit • Override customer prepayment_required flag (with reason, logged in audit) • Approve/reject add-item change requests (Section 13). Cannot approve cancellation or issue original-payment refund directly — Admin only.
Create walk-in orders • Apply approved promo codes • Clock in/out • Open register session

Full KDS operational actions (accept, reject/request cancel, set preparing, set ready, no-show, ETA adjust, assign driver, approve busy-driver override, start/complete delivery, enter delivery PIN, initiate refund request) • Cannot approve cancellation or issue refunds (Admin only) • Cannot issue credits, edit orders, or access financial data
View assigned deliveries (future driver app only) • No admin panel access

🚨 Single Source of Truth Rule: The role permission tables in Sections 10.24 and 26.4 are the canonical authority for who can do what. If any feature section elsewhere in this PRD describes a permission differently (e.g. says “Admin only” when the matrix says “Admin/Manager”), the permission matrix wins. Engineers must implement authorization from the matrix, not from casual mentions in feature descriptions. Any discrepancy found should be reported and the feature section corrected to match the matrix.

users.role gates actor type and endpoint class. employee_profiles.role governs fine-grained staff permissions for STAFF users only. ADMIN bypasses employee_profiles.role checks entirely (see Section 10.0 canonical auth rule).
users.role = STAFF is only used to gate customer vs staff endpoint access at the JWT layer.
See Section 10.0 for the full canonical auth rule.
All critical admin actions are tracked in admin_audit_logs (see Section 10 in the main schema doc).
Admin-configurable business rules panel. Changes take effect immediately.

📌 Data source mapping: Store hours and delivery hours are stored in location_hours (Section 10.32B). Financial thresholds, operational settings, and feature flags are stored in location_settings (Section 10.6). Promo defaults are configured per promo code in promo_codes (Section 10.16). The admin settings panel is a UI surface that reads and writes to these separate tables — there is no single “store settings” table.
Flat fee; free delivery threshold configurable
Stored in location_settings.first_order_discount_enabled; suppresses all is_first_order_only promos when false
Stored in location_settings.default_promo_stackable; applied as default when creating new promo codes
Number of no-shows before prepayment required (default 3)
Soft delete / archive instead of hard delete for: menu items, inventory, employees, drivers, promo codes
Confirmation modals required for: delete/archive, refund, credit issuance, no-sale drawer, close register, cancel order, assign busy driver
Global admin search across: orders, customers, employees, drivers, inventory, promos
CSV export available for: orders, employee hours, inventory, restock lists, driver history, support tickets, sales summaries
Status badges with consistent colour coding: order status, inventory status, employee shift, driver availability, ticket status, register status
Empty states for every list: clear message + primary action when no data
Keyboard shortcuts for POS: new order, mark ready, print receipt, cash payment, drawer open (with approval), search item
• All 8 dashboard widgets displayed on admin home screen with accurate live counts
• Sales, product performance, and employee reports accessible with date range filters
• Role-based access enforced server-side (not just frontend)
• Each role can only access the actions defined in 26.4
• All critical actions in 26.5 create audit log entries
• Audit log is read-only; never editable
• CSV export available on all list pages
• Archive used instead of hard delete on all entity types
• Confirmation modals shown for all destructive or financial actions
Wings 4 U will expand to multiple locations. The database and schema are designed for this from day one. No schema migrations are needed when location 2 opens.
Architecture: One shared Postgres DB, all locations
• All operational tables carry location_id FK → locations(id)
• Every query scoped by location_id at the application layer
• Each location has its own: menu, staff, drivers, register, KDS, inventory, settings
• Customer accounts are shared (a customer can order from any location)
• Credit wallets, loyalty (future), and promo codes are global by default (can be scoped per location later)
1️⃣ INSERT INTO locations → new location row
2️⃣ INSERT INTO location_settings → prep times, hours, delivery settings
3️⃣ Create admin user (users.role = ADMIN) + insert admin_location_assignments row for that location
4️⃣ Create menu, staff, drivers, register for that location
✔ No new tables, no migrations, no schema edits required
Admin Setup for 2 Locations (Schema Ready Now)
Schema is fully prepared. No changes needed when second admin / location is created.
• Two rows in users table with role = ADMIN
• Each admin linked to their location via admin_location_assignments (user_id, location_id)
• Admin panel endpoint reads admin_location_assignments to scope all queries
• Admin A (Location 1) sees: their orders, staff, drivers, inventory, KDS
• Admin B (Location 2) sees: their orders, staff, drivers, inventory, KDS
• Customer profiles visible to both (customers are shared)
❌ Super-Admin — DO NOT BUILD THIS FROM THIS PRD
A super-admin who can see all locations is a future need that is NOT designed yet.
This PRD does not contain a super-admin schema, permission model, or endpoint spec.
The sentence above only names a possible future approach — it is not a specification.
To build super-admin you would need, at minimum:
• A permission check layer (not in current role model)
• A decision on whether users.role gets a SUPER_ADMIN value or this is handled via a flag
• Cross-location data scoping rules defined per endpoint
• Audit log filtering that does not expose Location 2 data to Location 1 admin accidentally
For now: two separate location-scoped admins. That is the complete system.
No schema changes needed to onboard drivers/employees at location 2:
• drivers: driver_profiles.location_id assigns them to a home location
• employees: employee_profiles.location_id assigns their primary location
• An employee or driver can be reassigned by updating location_id
employee_profiles uses user_id as PK (one row per user). This means one employee profile per person.
If a future need arises where a single employee works across multiple locations:
→ Do NOT create multiple employee_profiles rows for the same user (breaks PK constraint)
→ Instead, add an employee_location_assignments join table:
For MVP: each employee has one location. The join table is a future-phase addition.
• All drivers created with is_active = true and availability_status = OFF_SHIFT
• Driver activates by clocking in at the POS (Section 23.6)
• This pattern works identically for location 1 and location 2
• Admin can manually override availability_status to AVAILABLE, UNAVAILABLE, or OFF_SHIFT from the driver profile at any time (ON_DELIVERY is system-derived only)
Each location has its own KDS screen(s) and POS terminal(s) registered in the devices table
KDS at Location 1 only shows orders with location_id = Location 1
POS WiFi restriction (Section 22.2) is enforced per location using that location's registered IP range
Printer assignments are per device, per location — no cross-location confusion
• All schema tables include location_id (see Section 10.32 for full canonical list)
• Opening location 2 requires only data entry (INSERT rows), zero schema migrations
• Two admins can be created from day 1; each scoped to their location
• Drivers and employees are location-scoped via profile FK
• Customer accounts are shared across locations

This section documents conscious trade-offs, known limitations, and areas that need re-evaluation before or during implementation. These are not blockers — they are things to be aware of so they do not surprise you mid-build.
📌 Recommended Document Split for Final Deliverables

This PRD carries product behavior, workflows, permissions, architecture guidance, schema reference, known drawbacks, and backlog in one file. That makes it comprehensive but heavy — heavy docs rot because one edit in one section does not always get reflected in every related section. For the

implementation phase, split into three living documents: (1) PRD — product behavior, UX flows, acceptance criteria, and the canonical permission matrix. (2) System Blueprint — architecture decisions, module boundaries, runtime behavior, middleware rules, device trust, and deployment notes. (3) Schema Spec — all table definitions, constraints, indexes, enums, and data-model invariants. Each document should cross-reference the others but own its own domain.
Friction for driver and staff; adds a step to every delivery
Ensures delivery authenticity without requiring a driver app. Bypassed by KDS employee with logged reason.
Stock truth is only as good as manual adjustments
Full recipe mapping requires significant effort. Manual system is stable and buildable first.
Guest checkout creates users row immediately
Required for no-show tracking, prepayment gate, and support tickets to work correctly.
Some customers may miss the window
Shorter window reduces kitchen disruption. Customer can always place a new order.
Cancel request from KDS by any employee (Admin approval required for finalization)
Restrict cancel to manager only (old model)

Operational speed with accountability. Any employee can request cancellation; Admin must approve before order is finalized as CANCELLED. Mirrors the refund request workflow. Audit trail captures requester, approver, reason, and timestamps.
Refund to original payment method disabled at launch
Admin can only issue store credit refunds initially
Payment gateway integration required. UI shows the option but disables it with a clear message.
Status updates missed; customer sees stale order state
Overdue delivery detection runs on background job
Up to 60-second lag before "almost there" message triggers
Acceptable for MVP. Reduce interval when driver app provides real-time pings.
POS WiFi IP allowlist is static
If store changes ISP or gets a dynamic IP, POS breaks
Store uses a static IP or sets up a router with a fixed
external IP. Config documented in setup guide.
Very busy kitchen may not notice new orders in 10 seconds
10 seconds is better than 3 but still tight. Consider audible alert or longer window as configurable setting.
A bad query without location_id filter could expose cross-location data
Enforce location_id scoping at the ORM/service layer. Add integration test for every cross-location leak.
Before coding starts, agree on this module boundary. A monolithic orders service becomes unmanageable fast.
Login, OTP, Google OAuth, sessions, logout, refresh
Location settings, hours, prep time, busy mode
Menu categories, items, modifiers, wing flavours, schedules
Cart state, ETA calculation, promo validation, total calculation
Place order, status lifecycle, order_status_events, snapshots
KDS-specific views, status updates, ETA adjust, no-show, cancellation request initiation, refund-request initiation, driver assignment UI, start/complete delivery UI, PIN entry UI
Driver profiles, availability, PIN validation, delivery history, driver-domain services (called by KDS for assignment/start/complete)
Payment capture, refund requests, store credit application
Promo codes, discount application, usage tracking
Item reviews, driver ratings, admin replies
Live order chat, message storage, read receipts
Stock items, adjustments, low-stock alerts, restock lists
Inquiry form submissions, admin status updates
Clock in/out, breaks, driver state sync, timesheet
Sessions, cash drawer events, receipt print logs
Sales, product performance, driver history, employee hours

The kds module owns all operational UI actions (cancellation requests, delivery controls including assign/start/complete for all employees, no-show). The drivers module owns driver state and delivery-specific domain logic (profiles, availability, PIN validation, delivery history). KDS delivery actions such as assign driver, start delivery, and complete delivery call drivers-domain services rather than duplicating driver business rules inside the KDS module. Do not duplicate permission checks across both modules.
